rules.title: NewID documentation
rules.body: Content to be added. NewIDs are member-level identifiers in the APCD. The same person can have multiple NewIDs across payers or years.

rules.title: StudyID documentation
rules.body: Content to be added. StudyIDs are de-identified person-level identifiers used for longitudinal tracking across claims.

rules.title: MPID documentation
rules.body: Content to be added. MPIDs are Master Person IDs used in mother-child linkage files and other cross-program matching.

rules.title: Avoid PROC SORT NODUPKEY as a general rule
rules.body: NODUPKEY silently drops rows and makes it hard to know what was lost. It is only appropriate when the deduplication grain is fully understood and intentional.

callout.title: Common mistake
callout.body: Using NODUPKEY to keep the highest income tier per studyid does NOT work as expected if income_rank is in the sort key — it keeps one row per studyid+income_rank pair, not one row per studyid.

rules.title: Use FIRST. DATA step logic for top-ranked row per ID
rules.body: Sort by studyid and the ranking variable, then use FIRST.studyid in a DATA step to retain exactly one record per ID.
list: Step 1 — Sort by studyid, then ranking variable (ascending or descending as needed)
Step 2 — Use a DATA step with FIRST.studyid logic to retain only the first record per studyid

rules.title: Enrollment span joins can multiply rows unexpectedly
rules.body: Many-to-many joins on enrollment spans produce fan-out — one member row can match multiple enrollment records, multiplying downstream counts. Always deduplicate before any aggregation step.
rules.tip: Check: if your row count after a join is higher than the distinct member count going in, fan-out has occurred. Investigate before continuing.
